summary: routecraft preinstall loads unpinned third-party dependency procwire@* on Windows under a fake native-addon cover
details: routecraft@5.0.0 is an Express look-alike whose package.json declares preinstall=`node./lib/configure.js` and lists `procwire: "*"` as a dependency. lib/configure.js (lines 10-12) gates on `os.platform() === 'win32' && v >= 18` and then calls `require('procwire')` inside a try/catch, printing a message about 'Skipping native addon compilation' as cover. The package ships no native addon sources (no binding.gyp, no C/C++ files), so the native-addon framing is misdirection. Because procwire is pinned to `*`, npm resolves to whatever the latest published version is at install time, and that package's top-level/require-time code executes inside the preinstall hook on every Windows install of routecraft. This gives the procwire publisher arbitrary code execution on installer machines on demand, with no version pinning or integrity check. The library code (lib/routecraft.js, application.js, request.js, response.js) is a near-verbatim Express clone, which serves as the social-engineering lure — installers think they are pulling a small Express alternative while preinstall fetches and runs unpinned third-party code on Windows.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
